name: Yukon (Canada)
description: Three vertical stripes of green, white, and blue (the central white panel being twice as wide as the others), with the Yukon coat of arms surmounted by a Malamute dog and resting on two sprigs of fireweed centred on the white panel.
history: Adopted on 1 December 1967 (Canada's centennial year) following a competition organised by the Yukon Branch of the Royal Canadian Legion in 1966; designed by Lynn Lambert, then a student in Destruction Bay.

features.symbolism: Green for the territory's forests, white for snow, and blue for its rivers and lakes.

features.role: Yukon coat of arms with two sprigs of fireweed beneath
features.symbolism: The arms (granted 1956) feature a Saint George's cross with a roundel of vair (heraldic fur) representing the fur trade, wavy bars for the Yukon and Klondike rivers, and red 'piles' representing the territory's mountains topped with gold roundels for mineral wealth; the surmounting Malamute dog stands for loyalty and endurance. Two sprigs of fireweed (the territorial floral emblem since 1957) are placed below.
